1:
"And he said unto Moses, Come up unto the LORD, thou, and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel; and worship ye afar off." The witnesses note the call up the mount to worship afar off. On "Come up unto the LORD, thou, and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders," the grammatical voices note the summons. Henry and Gill note that "he said unto Moses, Come up unto the LORD, thou, and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel" — God called Moses, with Aaron, his two sons Nadab and Abihu, and seventy elders, to come up the mount; the representatives (the chosen company, the heads of Israel, summoned to draw near); the honor (the elders called to a special nearness, the covenant about to be ratified, vv.9-11).

On "Come up unto the LORD," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the approach. Calvin and Ellicott note the call to come up (to draw near to God, the representatives admitted) — the approach to God; the privilege of the chosen.

On "worship ye afar off," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the distance: "worship ye afar off" — the worship at a distance (the elders to worship from afar, not coming as near as Moses, v.2); the reverence (the distance kept, the gradations of nearness, Moses alone admitted to the closest, vv.2); the providence (the reverent approach, the order of the covenant; a faint figure of the access opened in Christ, who brings near them that were afar off, Ephesians 2:13). The verse displays the call up the mount to worship afar off.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the summons — "Come up unto the LORD, thou, and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders... worship ye afar off," the call (vv.9-11, Henry, Gill); "Come up unto the LORD," the approach (Calvin, Ellicott); "worship ye afar off," the distance (Ephesians 2:13, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the summoned elders. The verse marks the call: come up. The verse displays the company: Aaron and the elders. The verse displays the worship: afar off. The verse displays the reverence: the distance. The call displays the representatives drawn near to worship. The verse displays the elders: summoned. The verse displays the LORD: approached. The verse displays the worship: reverent. The call up the mount to worship afar off displays the reverent approach: come up, and worship afar off (vv.9-11; Ephesians 2:13) — the representatives drawn near, yet the distance kept in reverence. The verse displays Moses alone coming near (v.2).
2:
"And Moses alone shall come near the LORD: but they shall not come nigh; neither shall the people go up with him." The very brief verse notes Moses alone admitted nearest to God. The grammatical voices note the distinction. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Moses alone shall come near the LORD: but they shall not come nigh; neither shall the people go up with him" — Moses alone was to come near to the LORD (to the closest), while the elders worshipped afar off (v.1) and the people did not go up at all; the gradations of nearness (Moses nearest, the elders farther, the people at the foot); the office of Moses (the mediator, admitted to the closest fellowship with God).

On "Moses alone shall come near the LORD," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the mediator. Calvin and Wesley note Moses alone coming near (the mediator, the closest access) — the office (Moses the go-between, the type of Christ, the one Mediator, who alone enters the holiest, Hebrews 9:24); the singular nearness.

On "they shall not come nigh; neither shall the people go up with him," the grammatical-historical note the distance: "they shall not come nigh; neither shall the people go up with him" — the elders and people kept back (the gradations of access, the reverence); the providence (the distance under the law, the mediator alone admitted, a figure of the access through Christ alone, John 14:6; Hebrews 10:19-20). The verse displays Moses alone admitted nearest to God.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the distinction — "Moses alone shall come near the LORD: but they shall not come nigh; neither shall the people go up with him," the gradations (v.1, Keil, Gill); "Moses alone shall come near the LORD," the mediator (Hebrews 9:24, Calvin, Wesley); "they shall not come nigh; neither shall the people go up with him," the distance (Hebrews 10:19-20, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the nearest Moses. The verse marks the access: Moses alone. The verse displays the elders: kept back. The verse displays the people: not up. The verse displays the mediator: nearest. The distinction displays Moses the mediator admitted nearest. The verse displays the Moses: near. The verse displays the people: afar. The verse displays the access: graded. Moses alone admitted nearest to God displays the office of the mediator: Moses alone shall come near (Hebrews 9:24; 10:19-20) — the mediator admitted to the closest fellowship, a type of Christ who alone enters the holiest. The verse displays the people's covenant promise (v.3).
3:
"And Moses came and told the people all the words of the LORD, and all the judgments: and all the people answered with one voice, and said, All the words which the LORD hath said will we do." The witnesses note the people's unanimous promise to keep the covenant. On "Moses came and told the people all the words of the LORD, and all the judgments," the grammatical voices note the rehearsal. Ellicott and Gill note that "Moses came and told the people all the words of the LORD, and all the judgments: and all the people answered with one voice... All the words which the LORD hath said will we do" — Moses rehearsed to the people all God's words and judgments (the covenant terms, the moral and judicial law, chs. 20-23), and the people, with one voice, promised to do them all; the covenant proposed (the law set before the people); the unanimous consent (the whole people, with one voice, engaging to obey).

On "Moses came and told the people all the words of the LORD," the reformed and witnesses note the rehearsal. Gill notes Moses telling all the words and judgments (the full covenant, faithfully) — the rehearsal; the covenant set before them.

On "all the people answered with one voice... All the words which the LORD hath said will we do," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the promise: "all the people answered with one voice... will we do" — the unanimous promise (the people engaging to obey, as Exodus 19:8); the covenant accepted (the people's vow, sincere yet self-confident, soon to fail, ch. 32; Deuteronomy 5:27-29); the providence (the covenant engaged, the law a schoolmaster, Galatians 3:24). The verse displays the people's unanimous promise to keep the covenant.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the promise — "Moses came and told the people all the words of the LORD... all the people answered with one voice... will we do," the covenant (Exodus 19:8, Ellicott, Gill); "Moses came and told the people all the words of the LORD," the rehearsal (Gill); "all the people answered with one voice... will we do," the promise (Galatians 3:24, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the promising people. The verse marks the rehearsal: all the words. The verse displays the consent: one voice. The verse displays the promise: we will do. The verse displays the covenant: engaged. The promise displays the people engaging to obey. The verse displays the people: promising. The verse displays the covenant: accepted. The verse displays the Moses: rehearsing. The people's unanimous promise to keep the covenant displays the engagement: all the words the LORD hath said will we do (Exodus 19:8; Galatians 3:24) — the people's vow, sincere yet self-confident, the covenant engaged. The verse displays the book of the covenant written (v.4).
4:
"And Moses wrote all the words of the LORD, and rose up early in the morning, and builded an altar under the hill, and twelve pillars, according to the twelve tribes of Israel." The witnesses note Moses writing the covenant and building the altar and pillars. On "Moses wrote all the words of the LORD," the grammatical voices note the writing. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Moses wrote all the words of the LORD, and rose up early in the morning, and builded an altar under the hill, and twelve pillars" — Moses wrote down all God's words (the book of the covenant, v.7), and early in the morning built an altar at the foot of the mount, with twelve pillars; the written covenant (the words recorded, the inspired Scripture); the altar (for the sacrifices of the covenant, vv.5-6); the twelve pillars (for the twelve tribes, the parties to the covenant).

On "builded an altar under the hill," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the altar. Calvin and Ellicott note the altar (for the covenant sacrifices, representing God, one party to the covenant) — the place of sacrifice; the meeting of God and the people in the blood.

On "twelve pillars, according to the twelve tribes of Israel," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the pillars: "twelve pillars, according to the twelve tribes of Israel" — the twelve pillars (representing the twelve tribes, the people, the other party to the covenant); the covenant between God (the altar) and the people (the pillars); the providence (the solemn ratification, the parties represented). The verse displays Moses writing the covenant and building the altar and pillars.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the preparation — "Moses wrote all the words of the LORD... builded an altar... and twelve pillars," the covenant ratified (vv.5-7, Keil, Gill); "builded an altar under the hill," the altar (Calvin, Ellicott); "twelve pillars, according to the twelve tribes of Israel," the pillars (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the preparing Moses. The verse marks the writing: the words. The verse displays the altar: built. The verse displays the pillars: twelve. The verse displays the covenant: ratified. The preparation displays the covenant between God and the people. The verse displays the Moses: writing. The verse displays the altar: for God. The verse displays the pillars: for the tribes. Moses writing the covenant and building the altar and pillars display the ratification: the words written, the altar and twelve pillars (vv.5-7) — the covenant between God (the altar) and the people (the pillars), solemnly ratified. The verse displays the burnt offerings and peace offerings (v.5).
5:
"And he sent young men of the children of Israel, which offered burnt offerings, and sacrificed peace offerings of oxen unto the LORD." The very brief verse notes the burnt offerings and peace offerings of the covenant. The grammatical voices note the sacrifices. Calvin and Gill note that "he sent young men of the children of Israel, which offered burnt offerings, and sacrificed peace offerings of oxen unto the LORD" — Moses sent young men (the firstborn, or chosen youths, before the Aaronic priesthood was established) to offer burnt offerings and peace offerings of oxen; the sacrifices of the covenant (the burnt offering for atonement and dedication, the peace offering for communion and fellowship); the blood (provided for the sprinkling, vv.6-8).

On "young men of the children of Israel," the witnesses note the offerers. Gill notes the young men (the firstborn, or chosen, acting as priests before Aaron's consecration) — the offerers; the sacrifices presented.

On "burnt offerings, and... peace offerings of oxen," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the offerings: "burnt offerings, and sacrificed peace offerings of oxen" — the burnt offering (wholly consumed, for atonement and dedication, Leviticus 1) and the peace offering (for communion, the covenant fellowship, Leviticus 3); the covenant sealed in blood and fellowship; the providence (the sacrifices the ground of the covenant, the blood for the sprinkling, vv.6-8; a type of Christ, the burnt offering and peace offering, the atonement and the peace, Ephesians 2:14-16). The verse displays the burnt offerings and peace offerings of the covenant.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the sacrifices — "young men... offered burnt offerings, and sacrificed peace offerings of oxen unto the LORD," the offerings (vv.6-8, Calvin, Gill); "young men of the children of Israel," the offerers (Gill); "burnt offerings, and... peace offerings of oxen," the offerings (Ephesians 2:14-16, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the covenant sacrifices. The verse marks the offerers: young men. The verse displays the burnt offering: for atonement. The verse displays the peace offering: for communion. The verse displays the covenant: sealed. The sacrifices display the covenant grounded in blood and fellowship. The verse displays the offerings: presented. The verse displays the blood: provided. The verse displays the Christ: the offering. The burnt offerings and peace offerings of the covenant display the covenant sacrifices: burnt offerings and peace offerings of oxen (vv.6-8; Ephesians 2:14-16) — the atonement and the communion, a type of Christ the offering. The verse displays the blood divided (v.6).
6:
"And Moses took half of the blood, and put it in basons; and half of the blood he sprinkled on the altar." The very brief verse notes the blood divided between the altar and the people. The grammatical voices note the division. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Moses took half of the blood, and put it in basons; and half of the blood he sprinkled on the altar" — Moses divided the blood of the sacrifices into two halves: half put in basins (for the people, vv.8), half sprinkled on the altar (for God); the dividing of the blood (the two parties to the covenant, God, represented by the altar, and the people, both sharing in the one blood); the covenant in blood (the blood the bond and seal of the covenant, vv.8).

On "half of the blood... put it in basons," the witnesses note the people's portion. Gill notes the half in basins (reserved for sprinkling on the people, vv.8) — the people's share; the blood for them.

On "half of the blood he sprinkled on the altar," the reformed and grammatical-historical note God's portion: "half of the blood he sprinkled on the altar" — the half on the altar (for God, the other party); the covenant in blood (the blood applied to both parties, God and the people, the bond of the covenant); the providence (the blood of the covenant, vv.8; a type of the blood of Christ, the blood of the new covenant, Matthew 26:28; Hebrews 9:18-22). The verse displays the blood divided between the altar and the people.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the division — "Moses took half of the blood, and put it in basons; and half of the blood he sprinkled on the altar," the dividing (vv.8, Keil, Gill); "half of the blood... put it in basons," the people's portion (Gill); "half of the blood he sprinkled on the altar," God's portion (Matthew 26:28; Hebrews 9:18-22, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the divided blood. The verse marks the blood: divided. The verse displays the basins: for the people. The verse displays the altar: for God. The verse displays the covenant: in blood. The division displays the blood applied to both parties. The verse displays the blood: in basins. The verse displays the altar: sprinkled. The verse displays the covenant: sealed. The blood divided between the altar and the people displays the covenant in blood: half in basins, half on the altar (vv.8; Hebrews 9:18-22) — the blood applied to both parties, the bond of the covenant, a type of the blood of Christ. The verse displays the book read (v.7).
7:
"And he took the book of the covenant, and read in the audience of the people: and they said, All that the LORD hath said will we do, and be obedient." The witnesses note the reading of the book of the covenant and the people's renewed promise. On "he took the book of the covenant, and read in the audience of the people," the grammatical voices note the reading. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "he took the book of the covenant, and read in the audience of the people: and they said, All that the LORD hath said will we do, and be obedient" — Moses read the book of the covenant (the words he had written, v.4) aloud to the people, and they renewed their promise: "All that the LORD hath said will we do, and be obedient"; the public reading (the covenant terms read aloud, the people hearing); the renewed consent (the people promising obedience, now adding "and be obedient," the fuller engagement).

On "the book of the covenant," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the book. Calvin and JFB note the "book of the covenant" (the written law, the inspired Scripture, the covenant document, cited Hebrews 9:19) — the written covenant; the basis of the engagement.

On "All that the LORD hath said will we do, and be obedient," the puritan and grammatical-historical note the promise: "will we do, and be obedient" — the renewed, fuller promise (the people engaging both to do and to obey, the full submission); the covenant accepted (yet, the witnesses note, in self-confidence, soon broken, ch. 32; Deuteronomy 5:29); the providence (the covenant ratified, the people's vow, the blood to seal it, v.8). The verse displays the reading of the book and the people's renewed promise.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the reading — "the book of the covenant, and read in the audience of the people... will we do, and be obedient," the covenant (Hebrews 9:19, Keil, Ellicott); "the book of the covenant," the book (Calvin, JFB); "All that the LORD hath said will we do, and be obedient," the promise (Deuteronomy 5:29, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the read covenant. The verse marks the book: read. The verse displays the people: hearing. The verse displays the promise: do and obey. The verse displays the covenant: accepted. The reading displays the covenant heard and accepted. The verse displays the book: of the covenant. The verse displays the people: promising. The verse displays the obedience: vowed. The reading of the book and the people's renewed promise display the covenant accepted: the book read, will we do and be obedient (Hebrews 9:19; Deuteronomy 5:29) — the covenant heard and accepted, the fuller engagement, soon to be sealed in blood. The verse displays the blood of the covenant (v.8).
8:
"And Moses took the blood, and sprinkled it on the people, and said, Behold the blood of the covenant, which the LORD hath made with you concerning all these words." The witnesses note the blood of the covenant sprinkled on the people — the crux of the covenant blood. On "Moses took the blood, and sprinkled it on the people," the grammatical voices note the sprinkling. Ellicott and Gill note that "Moses took the blood, and sprinkled it on the people, and said, Behold the blood of the covenant, which the LORD hath made with you" — Moses sprinkled the reserved half of the blood (v.6) on the people, declaring it "the blood of the covenant"; the sprinkling (the blood applied to the people, sealing them into the covenant); the covenant ratified in blood (the bond sealed, the people bound and consecrated by the blood).

On "Behold the blood of the covenant, which the LORD hath made with you," interpreters expound the great word. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the type — "Behold the blood of the covenant": the blood that sealed the old covenant a type of the blood of Christ, the blood of the new covenant; the witnesses note the New Testament fulfillment — the Lord, instituting the supper, took the cup and said, "This is my blood of the new testament (covenant), which is shed for many for the remission of sins" (Matthew 26:28; Mark 14:24; Luke 22:20, "the new testament in my blood"), echoing this very word; and the Epistle to the Hebrews expounds it at length — "for where a testament is, there must also of necessity be the death of the testator... whereupon neither the first testament was dedicated without blood. For when Moses had spoken every precept to all the people... he took the blood... and sprinkled both the book, and all the people, saying, This is the blood of the testament which God hath enjoined unto you" (Hebrews 9:16-22, "without shedding of blood is no remission"); the covenant sealed in blood (no covenant, no remission, without blood); the blood of Christ the blood of the everlasting covenant (Hebrews 13:20; 1 Peter 1:2, "the sprinkling of the blood of Jesus Christ"; 10:29).

On "concerning all these words," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the covenant: "concerning all these words" — the covenant of all the words (the law, the terms, sealed by the blood); the binding (the people bound to the whole covenant by the blood); the providence (the type of the new covenant in Christ's blood, the better covenant, Hebrews 8:6; 12:24). The verse displays the blood of the covenant sprinkled on the people.

The witnesses agree the substance: Moses sprinkled the blood on the people, declaring it the blood of the covenant (Ellicott, Calvin, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the covenant blood: "the blood of the covenant" — the blood sealing the old covenant, a type of the blood of Christ, the blood of the new covenant (Matthew 26:28; Hebrews 9:16-22), the covenant sealed in blood ("without shedding of blood is no remission"), the blood of Christ the blood of the everlasting covenant (Hebrews 13:20; 1 Peter 1:2). The verse displays the covenant-blood crux. The verse marks the blood: sprinkled. The verse displays the Christ: the blood. The blood displays the covenant sealed in the blood, a type of Christ's. The verse displays the covenant: in blood. The blood of the covenant sprinkled on the people displays the crux: behold the blood of the covenant (Matthew 26:28; Hebrews 9:16-22; 13:20; 1 Peter 1:2) — the type of the blood of Christ,, the new covenant sealed in his blood. The verse displays the vision of God (vv.9-11).
9:
"Then went up Moses, and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel:" The very brief verse notes the elders going up to God. The grammatical voices note the ascent. Henry and Gill note that "then went up Moses, and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel" — after the covenant was sealed in blood (v.8), Moses, Aaron, his sons, and the seventy elders went up the mount (as called, v.1) to the special nearness; the ascent (the representatives going up to God); the covenant fellowship (the sealed covenant opening the way to the vision and the meal, vv.10-11); the honor (the elders admitted to behold God).

On "went up Moses, and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu," the witnesses note the company. Gill notes the company going up (Moses, Aaron, his sons, the elders, as summoned, v.1) — the representatives; the ascent.

On "and seventy of the elders of Israel," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the elders: "and seventy of the elders of Israel" — the seventy elders (the representatives of the people, going up to behold God, vv.10-11); the covenant fellowship (the sealed covenant the ground of the nearness); the providence (the vision and the meal following the blood, the access through the covenant). The verse displays the elders going up to God.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the ascent — "then went up Moses, and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel," the company (v.1, Henry, Gill); "went up Moses, and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu," the company (Gill); "and seventy of the elders of Israel," the elders (vv.10-11, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the ascending elders. The verse marks the ascent: up to God. The verse displays the company: Moses and the elders. The verse displays the covenant: sealed. The verse displays the fellowship: opened. The ascent displays the representatives going up after the blood. The verse displays the elders: ascending. The verse displays the covenant: the ground. The verse displays the vision: at hand. The elders going up to God display the covenant fellowship: Moses, Aaron, and the elders went up (v.1; vv.10-11) — the representatives going up after the blood, the sealed covenant opening the way to the vision. The verse displays the vision of the God of Israel (v.10).
10:
"And they saw the God of Israel: and there was under his feet as it were a paved work of a sapphire stone, and as it were the body of heaven in his clearness." The witnesses note the vision of the God of Israel — the crux of seeing God. On "they saw the God of Israel," interpreters expound the vision. The reformed and the dominant reading note the wonder — "they saw the God of Israel": the elders, going up, "saw the God of Israel"; the witnesses weigh the manner of the vision — not that they saw God in his essence (for "no man hath seen God at any time," John 1:18; "thou canst not see my face: for there shall no man see me, and live," Exodus 33:20; "whom no man hath seen, nor can see," 1 Timothy 6:16), but they saw a visible manifestation, a glorious appearance, a representation of God's presence and majesty (the glory under his feet, vv.10b; perhaps the pre-incarnate Christ, the Word, the visible image of the invisible God, John 1:18, "the only begotten Son... he hath declared him"; the Angel of the covenant, in whom God revealed himself); the gracious condescension (God showing himself to the elders, in a manifestation they could bear); the covenant privilege (the sealed covenant opening the way to behold God, the foretaste of the beatific vision, the pure in heart shall see God, Matthew 5:8; 1 John 3:2).

On "under his feet as it were a paved work of a sapphire stone," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the glory. Wesley and Keil note the pavement under his feet (a sapphire-blue, transparent splendor, like the very body of heaven for clearness) — the glorious manifestation; the pavement of sapphire (the blue of heaven, the throne above the firmament, Ezekiel 1:26, "the likeness of a throne, as the appearance of a sapphire stone"; Revelation 4:3); the majesty of the appearance.

On "as it were the body of heaven in his clearness," the grammatical-historical note the clearness: "as it were the body of heaven in his clearness" — the clear, bright, transparent heaven (the splendor of the manifestation); the glory (the visible token of God's presence and majesty); the providence (the gracious vision granted to the covenant representatives, the foretaste of seeing God). The verse displays the vision of the God of Israel.

The witnesses agree the substance: the elders saw a glorious manifestation of the God of Israel, with the sapphire pavement under his feet (Wesley, Keil, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of seeing God: "they saw the God of Israel" — not the divine essence (John 1:18; Exodus 33:20; 1 Timothy 6:16), but a visible manifestation of his glory and majesty (perhaps the pre-incarnate Word, who declares the Father), a gracious condescension and covenant privilege, a foretaste of the beatific vision (Matthew 5:8; 1 John 3:2); the sapphire pavement the token of his throne (Ezekiel 1:26). The verse displays the seeing-God crux. The verse marks the vision: the God of Israel. The verse displays the manifestation: of glory. The verse displays the sapphire: the pavement. The verse displays the foretaste: of the vision. The vision displays God manifested to the covenant elders. The verse displays the God: seen. The verse displays the glory: beheld. The verse displays the Christ: the manifestation. The vision of the God of Israel displays the crux: they saw the God of Israel (John 1:18; Exodus 33:20; Ezekiel 1:26; Matthew 5:8) — not the essence, but a visible manifestation of glory, the covenant privilege, a foretaste of the beatific vision. The verse displays the covenant meal (v.11).
11:
"And upon the nobles of the children of Israel he laid not his hand: also they saw God, and did eat and drink." The witnesses note the covenant meal in the presence of God — the crux of the fellowship feast. On "upon the nobles of the children of Israel he laid not his hand," the grammatical voices note the sparing. Calvin and Gill note that "upon the nobles of the children of Israel he laid not his hand: also they saw God, and did eat and drink" — God "laid not his hand" upon the nobles (the elders), that is, did not strike or destroy them, though they saw him (for sinful man could not ordinarily behold God and live, Exodus 33:20; Judges 13:22); the gracious sparing (the elders, beholding God, preserved by his mercy, not consumed); the wonder (that sinful men saw God and lived).

On "also they saw God," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the vision. Calvin and Ellicott note that "they saw God" (the manifestation, v.10) and were spared — the gracious vision; the mercy that preserved them.

On "and did eat and drink," interpreters expound the meal. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and the dominant reading note the covenant meal — "did eat and drink": the elders ate and drank (of the peace offerings, the covenant sacrifices, v.5) in the presence of God; the witnesses note the meaning — the covenant feast, the fellowship meal that sealed the covenant (the eating before God a token of reconciliation and communion, the covenant ratified in a shared meal, as covenants were sealed with a feast, Genesis 31:54; the peace offering eaten in fellowship with God); the lesson — the communion of the covenant (the reconciled people feasting in God's presence, a foretaste of the marriage supper, Revelation 19:9; the Lord's table, the communion of the new covenant, 1 Corinthians 10:16-21; the eating and drinking in the kingdom, Luke 22:30); the peace and joy of the covenant fellowship. The verse displays the covenant meal in the presence of God.

The witnesses agree the substance: the elders saw God, were spared, and ate and drank before him (Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the fellowship feast: "they saw God, and did eat and drink" — the elders graciously spared though they beheld God (Exodus 33:20), and the covenant meal, the fellowship feast that sealed the covenant (the peace offerings eaten before God, Genesis 31:54), the communion of the reconciled people, a foretaste of the marriage supper and the Lord's table (Revelation 19:9; 1 Corinthians 10:16-21; Luke 22:30). The verse displays the covenant-meal crux. The verse marks the sparing: not consumed. The verse displays the vision: they saw God. The verse displays the meal: ate and drank. The verse displays the fellowship: the feast. The meal displays the covenant communion in God's presence. The verse displays the elders: spared. The verse displays the feast: shared. The verse displays the communion: with God. The covenant meal in the presence of God displays the crux: they saw God, and did eat and drink (Exodus 33:20; Genesis 31:54; Revelation 19:9; 1 Corinthians 10:16-21) — the gracious sparing, the fellowship feast of the covenant, the communion of the reconciled, a foretaste of the marriage supper. The verse displays the call up for the tables (v.12).
12:
"And the LORD said unto Moses, Come up to me into the mount, and be there: and I will give thee tables of stone, and a law, and commandments which I have written; that thou mayest teach them." The witnesses note God calling Moses up for the tables of the law. On "Come up to me into the mount, and be there," the grammatical voices note the call. Henry and Gill note that "the LORD said unto Moses, Come up to me into the mount, and be there: and I will give thee tables of stone, and a law, and commandments which I have written; that thou mayest teach them" — God called Moses higher up the mount, to remain there (the forty days, v.18), to receive the tables of stone with the law and commandments God had written; the call (Moses summoned to the longer, closer communion); the tables (the stone tables of the Ten Commandments, written by God, Exodus 31:18; 32:16).

On "I will give thee tables of stone, and a law, and commandments which I have written," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the writing. Calvin and Ellicott note the tables of stone (the Ten Commandments, written by God's own finger, Exodus 31:18, "written with the finger of God"; 32:16, "the writing of God, graven upon the tables") — the divine authorship (the law God's own writing); the permanence (graven in stone, the enduring moral law).

On "that thou mayest teach them," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the purpose: "that thou mayest teach them" — the law given to Moses to teach the people (the mediator receiving and teaching the law); the providence (the moral law, written by God, given for the instruction of the people, the abiding rule, Matthew 5:17-19; the law written by God on stone, the new covenant law written on the heart, Jeremiah 31:33; 2 Corinthians 3:3). The verse displays God calling Moses up for the tables of the law.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the call — "Come up to me into the mount... I will give thee tables of stone, and a law... that thou mayest teach them," the tables (Exodus 31:18, Henry, Gill); "I will give thee tables of stone, and a law, and commandments which I have written," the writing (Exodus 32:16, Calvin, Ellicott); "that thou mayest teach them," the purpose (Jeremiah 31:33, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the calling LORD. The verse marks the call: come up. The verse displays the tables: of stone. The verse displays the law: God's writing. The verse displays the teaching: the purpose. The call displays God giving the written law. The verse displays the Moses: summoned. The verse displays the law: written by God. The verse displays the people: to be taught. God calling Moses up for the tables of the law displays the giving of the law: come up, I will give thee the tables, that thou mayest teach them (Exodus 31:18; 32:16; Jeremiah 31:33) — the moral law written by God on stone, given for the people's instruction. The verse displays Moses and Joshua going up (v.13).
13:
"And Moses rose up, and his minister Joshua: and Moses went up into the mount of God." The very brief verse notes Moses and Joshua going up the mount of God. The grammatical voices note the ascent. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Moses rose up, and his minister Joshua: and Moses went up into the mount of God" — Moses rose, with his minister (attendant, servant) Joshua, and went up into the mount of God; the ascent (Moses going up for the forty days, vv.18); Joshua his minister (the faithful attendant, the future leader, accompanying Moses part of the way); the mount of God (Sinai, the place of the law).

On "his minister Joshua," the witnesses note the attendant. Gill notes Joshua, Moses' minister (the faithful servant and attendant, accompanying him) — the minister; the future leader trained in attendance on Moses.

On "Moses went up into the mount of God," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the ascent: "Moses went up into the mount of God" — Moses going up (to the closer communion, the forty days, vv.18); the mount of God (Sinai, Exodus 3:1); the providence (the mediator going up to receive the law, leaving Joshua, perhaps, on the way, vv.14). The verse displays Moses and Joshua going up the mount of God.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the ascent — "Moses rose up, and his minister Joshua: and Moses went up into the mount of God," the ascent (vv.18, Keil, Gill); "his minister Joshua," the attendant (Gill); "Moses went up into the mount of God," the ascent (Exodus 3:1, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the ascending Moses. The verse marks the ascent: up the mount. The verse displays the Joshua: the minister. The verse displays the mount: of God. The verse displays the communion: the forty days. The ascent displays Moses going up to God. The verse displays the Moses: ascending. The verse displays the Joshua: attending. The verse displays the law: to be received. Moses and Joshua going up the mount of God display the ascent to communion: Moses and Joshua went up into the mount of God (Exodus 3:1; vv.18) — the mediator going up to receive the law, the faithful Joshua attending. The verse displays the charge to the elders (v.14).
14:
"And he said unto the elders, Tarry ye here for us, until we come again unto you: and, behold, Aaron and Hur are with you: if any man have any matters to do, let him come unto them." The very brief verse notes the elders charged to govern in Moses' absence. The grammatical voices note the charge. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "he said unto the elders, Tarry ye here for us, until we come again unto you: and, behold, Aaron and Hur are with you: if any man have any matters to do, let him come unto them" — Moses charged the elders to wait until he returned, leaving Aaron and Hur in charge to handle any matters (disputes, business); the provision for government (the people not left without order in Moses' absence); the deputies (Aaron and Hur appointed to judge and govern, vv.18); the orderly arrangement.

On "Tarry ye here for us, until we come again unto you," the witnesses note the charge. Gill notes the elders charged to wait (until Moses returned) — the order; the people kept in their place.

On "Aaron and Hur are with you: if any man have any matters to do, let him come unto them," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the deputies: "Aaron and Hur are with you" — the deputies (Aaron and Hur, to govern and judge in Moses' absence, as in the battle with Amalek, Exodus 17:10-12); the provision (the people's matters handled, the order maintained); the providence (the government provided for, yet the absence the occasion of the golden calf, ch. 32, when the people corrupted themselves). The verse displays the elders charged to govern in Moses' absence.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the charge — "Tarry ye here... Aaron and Hur are with you: if any man have any matters to do, let him come unto them," the deputies (Exodus 17:10-12, Keil, Ellicott); "Tarry ye here for us, until we come again unto you," the charge (Gill); "Aaron and Hur are with you," the deputies (ch. 32, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the charging Moses. The verse marks the charge: tarry here. The verse displays the deputies: Aaron and Hur. The verse displays the matters: to them. The verse displays the order: maintained. The charge displays the government provided in Moses' absence. The verse displays the elders: charged. The verse displays the deputies: appointed. The verse displays the order: kept. The elders charged to govern in Moses' absence display the provision for order: Aaron and Hur are with you, bring your matters to them (Exodus 17:10-12) — the people's government provided, the order maintained while Moses was on the mount. The verse displays the cloud covering the mount (v.15).
15:
"And Moses went up into the mount, and a cloud covered the mount." The very brief verse notes Moses entering the cloud on the mount. The grammatical voices note the cloud. Ellicott and Gill note that "Moses went up into the mount, and a cloud covered the mount" — Moses went up, and a cloud covered the mount; the cloud (the visible token of God's presence, the glory veiled, Exodus 19:9, 16; the cloud into which Moses entered, vv.18); the presence of God (the cloud covering the mount, the glory descending); the prelude to the closer communion (the cloud the veil of the divine presence).

On "Moses went up into the mount," the witnesses note the ascent. Gill notes Moses going up (to the closer communion, the forty days) — the ascent; the mediator entering the presence.

On "a cloud covered the mount," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the cloud: "a cloud covered the mount" — the cloud (the token of God's presence, the glory veiled, Exodus 19:16); the covering (the mount wrapped in the cloud, the glory within, vv.16-17); the providence (the cloud the veil of the divine presence, into which Moses would enter, vv.18; a figure of the cloud of glory, the shekinah, that would fill the tabernacle, Exodus 40:34). The verse displays Moses entering the cloud on the mount.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the cloud — "Moses went up into the mount, and a cloud covered the mount," the presence (Exodus 19:16, Ellicott, Gill); "Moses went up into the mount," the ascent (Gill); "a cloud covered the mount," the cloud (Exodus 40:34, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the covered mount. The verse marks the ascent: up the mount. The verse displays the cloud: covering. The verse displays the presence: of God. The verse displays the glory: veiled. The cloud displays God's presence veiling the mount. The verse displays the Moses: ascending. The verse displays the cloud: descending. The verse displays the glory: within. Moses entering the cloud on the mount displays God's veiled presence: a cloud covered the mount (Exodus 19:16; 40:34) — the token of God's presence, the glory veiled, into which Moses would enter. The verse displays the glory of the LORD (vv.16-17).
16:
"And the glory of the LORD abode upon mount Sinai, and the cloud covered it six days: and the seventh day he called unto Moses out of the midst of the cloud." The witnesses note the glory of the LORD abiding on the mount. On "the glory of the LORD abode upon mount Sinai, and the cloud covered it six days," the grammatical voices note the glory. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the glory of the LORD abode upon mount Sinai, and the cloud covered it six days: and the seventh day he called unto Moses out of the midst of the cloud" — the glory of the LORD (the shekinah, the visible splendor of God's presence) abode on Sinai, the cloud covering it six days, and on the seventh day God called Moses from the midst of the cloud; the abiding glory (God's presence resting on the mount); the six days' waiting (Moses, perhaps, waiting six days before being called in); the seventh-day call (the sabbath, or the appointed day, God calling Moses into the cloud).

On "the glory of the LORD abode upon mount Sinai," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the presence. Calvin and Ellicott note the glory of the LORD (the shekinah, the visible manifestation of God's presence, Exodus 40:34; the glory that filled the mount) — the abiding presence; the majesty of God.

On "the seventh day he called unto Moses out of the midst of the cloud," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the call: "the seventh day he called unto Moses out of the midst of the cloud" — God calling Moses (on the seventh day, into the very midst of the cloud, the closest communion, vv.18); the call into the presence (the mediator admitted into the cloud of glory); the providence (the closer communion, the receiving of the law, vv.18; the waiting and the call). The verse displays the glory of the LORD abiding on the mount.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the glory — "the glory of the LORD abode upon mount Sinai... the seventh day he called unto Moses out of the midst of the cloud," the presence (Exodus 40:34, Keil, Gill); "the glory of the LORD abode upon mount Sinai," the presence (Calvin, Ellicott); "the seventh day he called unto Moses out of the midst of the cloud," the call (vv.18, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the abiding glory. The verse marks the glory: on Sinai. The verse displays the cloud: six days. The verse displays the call: the seventh day. The verse displays the communion: closest. The glory displays God's presence abiding on the mount. The verse displays the glory: abiding. The verse displays the Moses: called in. The verse displays the cloud: the veil. The glory of the LORD abiding on the mount displays God's presence: the glory abode on Sinai, the seventh day he called Moses (Exodus 40:34; vv.18) — the abiding shekinah, the mediator called into the cloud of glory. The verse displays the devouring fire (v.17).
17:
"And the sight of the glory of the LORD was like devouring fire on the top of the mount in the eyes of the children of Israel." The witnesses note the glory of the LORD like devouring fire — the crux of the consuming majesty. On "the sight of the glory of the LORD was like devouring fire on the top of the mount," interpreters expound the glory. The reformed and the dominant reading note the appearance — "the sight of the glory of the LORD was like devouring fire on the top of the mount in the eyes of the children of Israel": to the people below, the glory of the LORD on the top of the mount appeared like a devouring (consuming) fire; the witnesses note the meaning — the visible glory of God (the shekinah) appearing as fire, the token of his holiness, majesty, and consuming justice; the lesson — God a consuming fire (the holiness that devours sin and the sinner, Deuteronomy 4:24, "the LORD thy God is a consuming fire, even a jealous God"; 9:3; Hebrews 12:29, "our God is a consuming fire"); the terror of the divine majesty (the glory dreadful to the people, the holiness before which sinful man cannot stand); the contrast (the devouring fire to the people below, the gracious vision to Moses within, vv.18; the same glory terrible to the unreconciled, gracious to the mediator and the reconciled); the awe of the holy God.

On "in the eyes of the children of Israel," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the people's view. Wesley and Ellicott note the people seeing the glory as devouring fire (the dreadful appearance to those below) — the terror; the majesty that kept them at a distance (Exodus 19:12, 21).

On the fire, the grammatical-historical note the holiness: the devouring fire — the holiness and consuming justice of God (Deuteronomy 4:24; Hebrews 12:29); the providence (the glory terrible to the people, the law given amid the consuming fire, the contrast with the grace of the gospel, Hebrews 12:18-29). The verse displays the glory of the LORD like devouring fire.

The witnesses agree the substance: the glory of the LORD on the mount appeared to the people like devouring fire (Wesley, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the consuming majesty: "the glory of the LORD was like devouring fire" — the visible glory (the shekinah) appearing as consuming fire, the token of God's holiness, majesty, and justice (Deuteronomy 4:24; Hebrews 12:29, "our God is a consuming fire"); the terror of the divine majesty before which sinful man cannot stand, the glory dreadful to the people below yet gracious to the mediator within (vv.18). The verse displays the consuming-majesty crux. The verse marks the glory: like fire. The verse displays the fire: devouring. The verse displays the holiness: consuming. The verse displays the terror: of majesty. The fire displays the consuming holiness of God's glory. The verse displays the glory: dreadful. The verse displays the God: a consuming fire. The verse displays the people: in awe. The glory of the LORD like devouring fire displays the crux: the glory was like devouring fire (Deuteronomy 4:24; Hebrews 12:29) — the consuming holiness and majesty of God, terrible to the people below, gracious to the mediator within. The verse displays Moses forty days in the mount (v.18).
18:
"And Moses went into the midst of the cloud, and gat him up into the mount: and Moses was in the mount forty days and forty nights." The witnesses note Moses entering the cloud and abiding forty days. On "Moses went into the midst of the cloud, and gat him up into the mount," the grammatical voices note the entrance. Ellicott and Gill note that "Moses went into the midst of the cloud, and gat him up into the mount: and Moses was in the mount forty days and forty nights" — Moses entered the very midst of the cloud (the glory, the consuming fire, vv.17), going up higher, and remained on the mount forty days and forty nights; the entrance (the mediator going into the cloud of glory, where the people saw devouring fire, yet Moses graciously admitted); the forty days (the long communion, the receiving of the law and the pattern of the tabernacle, chs. 25-31).

On "Moses went into the midst of the cloud," the reformed and witnesses note the entrance. Gill notes Moses entering the cloud (the glory, the consuming fire to the people, yet Moses admitted and preserved) — the gracious admission; the mediator in the presence (the type of Christ entering the holiest, Hebrews 9:24).

On "Moses was in the mount forty days and forty nights," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the forty days: "forty days and forty nights" — the forty days (the long communion, Moses fasting, Deuteronomy 9:9, "I abode in the mount forty days and forty nights, I neither did eat bread nor drink water"); the receiving of the law and the tabernacle pattern (chs. 25-31); the witnesses note the parallel — the forty days of Moses (twice, Deuteronomy 9:18), of Elijah (1 Kings 19:8), and of Christ (Matthew 4:2, the forty days' fast); the providence (the mediator long in the presence, receiving the revelation for the people). The verse displays Moses entering the cloud and abiding forty days.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the entrance — "Moses went into the midst of the cloud... and was in the mount forty days and forty nights," the communion (Deuteronomy 9:9, Ellicott, Gill); "Moses went into the midst of the cloud," the entrance (Hebrews 9:24, Gill); "forty days and forty nights," the forty days (Matthew 4:2, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the entering Moses. The verse marks the entrance: into the cloud. The verse displays the mount: ascended. The verse displays the days: forty. The verse displays the communion: long. The entrance displays the mediator in the cloud of glory. The verse displays the Moses: within. The verse displays the cloud: entered. The verse displays the law: to be received. Moses entering the cloud and abiding forty days display the long communion: Moses went into the cloud, forty days and forty nights (Deuteronomy 9:9; Matthew 4:2; Hebrews 9:24) — the mediator graciously admitted into the glory, the long communion to receive the law and the tabernacle pattern. The verse closes the chapter, before the pattern of the tabernacle (ch.25).
